request.question: Activated charcoal and zeolites are both categorized by having pores equal to or smaller than what size?

title: Prussian blue (medical use)
Capillary fringe
Cigarette filter
Activated charcoal (medication)
Nikolay Zelinsky
Trichloroacetyl chloride
Pore (bread)
Nanoporous materials
Molecular sieve
Activated carbon
sentences: Prussian blue, also known as potassium ferric hexacyanoferrate, is used as a medication to treat thallium poisoning or radioactive cesium poisoning.
For thallium it may be used in addition to gastric lavage, activated charcoal, forced diuresis, and hemodialysis.
It is given by mouth or nasogastric tube.
Prussian blue is also used in the urine to test for G6PD deficiency.
The capillary fringe is the subsurface layer in which groundwater seeps up from a water table by capillary action to fill pores.
Pores at the base of the capillary fringe are filled with water due to tension saturation.
This saturated portion of the capillary fringe is less than total capillary rise because of the presence of a mix in pore size.
If pore size is small and relatively uniform, it is possible that soils can be completely saturated with water for several feet above the water table.
Alternately, the saturated portion will extend only a few inches above the water table when pore size is large.
Capillary action supports a vadose zone above the saturated base within which water content decreases with distance above the water table.
In soils with a wide range in pore size, the unsaturated zone can be several times thicker than the saturated zone.
A cigarette filter is a component of a cigarette, along with cigarette paper, capsules and adhesives.
The filter may be made from cellulose acetate fibre, paper or activated charcoal (either as a cavity filter or embedded into the cellulose acetate).
Macroporous phenol-formaldehyde resins and asbestos have also been used in cigarette filters.
The acetate and paper modify the particulate smoke phase by particle retention (filtration), and finely divided carbon modifies the gaseous phase (adsorption).

Filters can reduce "tar" and nicotine smoke yields up to 50%, with a greater removal rate for other classes of compounds (e.g., phenols), but are ineffective in filtering toxins such as carbon monoxide.
Most factory-made cigarettes are equipped with a filter; those who roll their own can buy them from a tobacconist.
Activated charcoal, also known as activated carbon, is a medication used to treat poisonings that occurred by mouth.
To be effective it must be used within a short time of the poisoning occurring, typically an hour.
It does not work for poisonings by cyanide, corrosive agents, iron, lithium, alcohols, or malathion.
It may be taken by mouth or given by a nasogastric tube.
Other uses include inside hemoperfusion machines.
Zelinsky studied at the University of Odessa and at the universities of Leipzig and Göttingen in Germany.
Zelinsky was one of the founders of theory on organic catalysis.
He is the inventor of the first effective filtering activated charcoal gas mask in the world (1915).
Trichloroacetyl chloride is the acyl chloride of trichloroacetic acid.
It can be formed by reacting chlorine with acetyl chloride or acetaldehyde in the presence of activated charcoal.
It is used in the manufacture of pharmaceuticals and plant protection compounds.
Pores are the air pockets found in leavened bread, where carbon dioxide from the fermentation process creates a network of primarily interconnected void structures.
The degree to which pores form are a major determiner in the texture ("crumb") of the bread.
Pore size varies between varieties of bread.
Sourdough bread is a variety with larger pores.
Rye bread has smaller pores and a denser crumb.
Nanoporous materials consist of a regular organic or inorganic framework supporting a regular, porous structure.
The size of the pores is generally 100 nanometers or smaller.
Most nanoporous materials can be classified as bulk materials or membranes.
Activated carbon and zeolites are two examples of bulk nanoporous materials, while cell membranes can be thought of as nanoporous membranes.
A molecular sieve is a material with pores (very small holes) of uniform size.
These pore diameters are similar in size to small molecules, and thus large molecules cannot enter or be absorbed, while smaller molecules can.

As a mixture of molecules migrate through the stationary bed of porous, semi-solid substance referred to as a sieve (or matrix), the components of highest molecular weight (which are unable to pass into the molecular pores) leave the bed first, followed by successively smaller molecules.
Some molecular sieves are used in chromatography, a separation technique that sorts molecules based on their size.
Other molecular sieves are used as desiccants (some examples include activated charcoal and silica gel).

Activated carbon, also called activated charcoal, is a form of carbon processed to have small, low-volume pores that increase the surface area available for adsorption or chemical reactions. "Activated" is sometimes substituted with "active".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: Activated charcoal and zeolites are both categorized by having pores equal to or smaller than what size?
hop1_docs: Molecular sieve | A molecular sieve is a material with pores (very small holes) of uniform size.  These pore diameters are similar in size to small molecules, and thus large molecules cannot enter or be absorbed, while smaller molecules can.  As a mixture of molecules migrate through the stationary bed of porous, semi-solid substance referred to as a sieve (or matrix), the components of highest molecular weight (which are unable to pass into the molecular pores) leave the bed first, followed by successively smaller molecules.  Some molecular sieves are used in chromatography, a separation technique that sorts molecules based on their size.  Other molecular sieves are used as desiccants (some examples include activated charcoal and silica gel).

Mesoporous organosilica | Mesoporous organosilica (periodic mesoporous organosilicas, PMO) are a type of silica containing organic groups that give rise to mesoporosity.  They exhibit pore size ranging from 2 nm - 50 nm, depending on the organic substituents.  In contrast, zeolites exhibit pore sizes less than a nanometer.  PMOs have potential applications as catalysts, adsorbents, trapping agents, drug delivery agents, stationary phases in chromatography and chemical sensors.

Nanoporous materials | Nanoporous materials consist of a regular organic or inorganic framework supporting a regular, porous structure.  The size of the pores is generally 100 nanometers or smaller.  Most nanoporous materials can be classified as bulk materials or membranes.  Activated carbon and zeolites are two examples of bulk nanoporous materials, while cell membranes can be thought of as nanoporous membranes.

Activated carbon | Activated carbon, also called activated charcoal, is a form of carbon processed to have small, low-volume pores that increase the surface area available for adsorption or chemical reactions. "Activated" is sometimes substituted with "active".

Catamaran | A catamaran ( ) (informally, a "cat") is a multi-hulled watercraft featuring two parallel hulls of equal size.  It is a geometry-stabilized craft, deriving its stability from its wide beam, rather than from a ballasted keel as with a monohull sailboat.  Being ballast-free and therefore lighter than a monohull, catamarans often have a shallower draft (draught) than comparably-sized monohulls.  The two hulls combined also often have a smaller hydrodynamic resistance than comparable monohulls, requiring less propulsive power from either sails or motors.  The catamaran's wider stance on the water can reduce both heeling and wave-induced motion, as compared with a monohull, and can give reduced wakes.
Microporous material | A microporous material is a material containing pores with diameters less than 2 nm.  Examples of microporous materials include zeolites and metal-organic frameworks.

Hyaena | Hyaena is a genus comprising two of the living species of hyenas: the striped hyena ("Hyaena hyaena") from western Asia and northern Africa and the brown hyena ("Hyaena brunnea") from southern Africa.  The brown hyena has sometimes been placed in a separate genus "Parahyaena", or even included in the otherwise fossil genus "Pachycrocuta", but recent sources have tended to place it in "Hyaena".  The brown hyena's skull is larger than that of the striped hyena.  The male brown hyena is slightly larger than the female, while the sexes of the striped hyena are equally sized.  Both species are smaller than the spotted hyena, but larger than the aardwolf.  They are predominantly scavengers.
response.summary_1:
- [3] "pores ... 100 nanometers or smaller" — nanoporous materials defined as generally ≤100 nm.  
- [3] Activated carbon and zeolites are listed as examples of "bulk nanoporous materials."  
- [6] "pores with diameters less than 2 nm" — microporous materials defined as <2 nm.  
- [6] Zeolites are given as an example of microporous materials.  
- [2] Zeolites "exhibit pore sizes less than a nanometer."  
- [4] Activated carbon described as having "small, low-volume pores" (no numeric size given).  
Bridge entities and ambiguities preventing a final answer:
- Bridge entities: "activated charcoal" (activated carbon) and "zeolites."  
- Ambiguity: passages classify both as "nanoporous (≤100 nm)" [3], but zeolites are also classified as "microporous (<2 nm / <1 nm)" [6][2]; activated carbon’s placement into microporous vs meso/macropore ranges is not specified numerically here. Therefore the single numeric threshold that applies to both (e.g., 100 nm vs 2 nm) is unresolved.
Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve ambiguity:
1) Fetch the "Activated carbon" page — extract "Pore size", "Pore structure", or "Porosity" section; get numeric pore-size ranges and classification (fractions of micropores vs mesopores; statement whether it's considered microporous).  
2) Fetch the "Zeolite" page — extract "Structure", "Pore size" or "Framework" section; get typical pore diameters and explicit classification (microporous) with numbers.  
3) Fetch the "Microporous material" page (or the full microporous materials article) — confirm definition "<2 nm" and example list (verify if activated carbon is listed as example).  
4) (If needed) Fetch "Nanoporous materials" page — confirm definition "100 nanometers or smaller" and context for classifying activated carbon and zeolites as nanoporous.  

Do not answer yet — need pages above (especially activated carbon and zeolite) to resolve which single pore-size threshold the question intends.
response.hop2_query: What pore-size classification applies to activated carbon and zeolites?
hop2_docs: Nanoporous materials | Nanoporous materials consist of a regular organic or inorganic framework supporting a regular, porous structure.  The size of the pores is generally 100 nanometers or smaller.  Most nanoporous materials can be classified as bulk materials or membranes.  Activated carbon and zeolites are two examples of bulk nanoporous materials, while cell membranes can be thought of as nanoporous membranes.

Carbide-derived carbon | Carbide-derived carbon (CDC), also known as tunable nanoporous carbon, is the common term for carbon materials derived from carbide precursors, such as binary (e.g. SiC, TiC), or ternary carbides, also known as MAX phases (e.g., TiAlC, TiSiC).  CDCs have also been derived from polymer-derived ceramics such as Si-O-C or Ti-C, and carbonitrides, such as Si-N-C.  CDCs can occur in various structures, ranging from amorphous to crystalline carbon, from sp- to sp-bonded, and from highly porous to fully dense.  Among others, the following carbon structures have been derived from carbide precursors: micro- and mesoporous carbon, amorphous carbon, carbon nanotubes, onion-like carbon, nanocrystalline diamond, graphene, and graphite.  Among carbon materials, microporous CDCs exhibit some of the highest reported specific surface areas (up to more than 3000 m/g).  By varying the type of the precursor and the CDC synthesis conditions, microporous and mesoporous structures with controllable average pore size and pore size distributions can be produced.  Depending on the precursor and the synthesis conditions, the average pore size control can be applied at sub-Angstrom accuracy.  This ability to precisely tune the size and shapes of pores makes CDCs attractive for selective sorption and storage of liquids and gases (e.g., hydrogen, methane, CO) and the high electric conductivity and electrochemical stability allows these structures to be effectively implemented in electrical energy storage and capacitive water desalinization.

Mesoporous organosilica | Mesoporous organosilica (periodic mesoporous organosilicas, PMO) are a type of silica containing organic groups that give rise to mesoporosity.  They exhibit pore size ranging from 2 nm - 50 nm, depending on the organic substituents.  In contrast, zeolites exhibit pore sizes less than a nanometer.  PMOs have potential applications as catalysts, adsorbents, trapping agents, drug delivery agents, stationary phases in chromatography and chemical sensors.

Activated carbon | Activated carbon, also called activated charcoal, is a form of carbon processed to have small, low-volume pores that increase the surface area available for adsorption or chemical reactions. "Activated" is sometimes substituted with "active".

Pore (bread) | Pores are the air pockets found in leavened bread, where carbon dioxide from the fermentation process creates a network of primarily interconnected void structures.  The degree to which pores form are a major determiner in the texture ("crumb") of the bread.  Pore size varies between varieties of bread.  Sourdough bread is a variety with larger pores.  Rye bread has smaller pores and a denser crumb.

Capillary flow porometry | Capillary flow porometry, also known as porometry, is a characterization technique based on the displacement of a wetting liquid from the sample pores by applying a gas at increasing pressure.  It is widely used to measure minimum, maximum (or first bubble point) and mean flow pore sizes, and pore size distribution of the through pores in membranes nonwovens, paper, filtration and ultrafiltration media, hollow fibers, ceramics, etc.

Calcilutite | Calcilutite (also known as cementstone) is a type of limestone that is composed of predominantly, more than 50 percent, of either clay-size or both silt-size and clay-size detrital (transported) carbonate grains.  These grains consist either of fossil fragments, ooids, intraclasts, pellets, other grains, or some combination of them.  The term calcilutite was originally proposed in 1903 by Grabau as a part of his calcilutite, calcarenite and calcirudite classification system based upon the size of the detrital grains composing a limestone.  In the original classification of limestone according dominant grain-size, calcisiltites were not named and are classified as calcilutite.  In this classification, which the majority of geologists follow, a calcilutite consists of both silt- and clay-size, less than 0.062 mm in diameter, grains.  It is the carbonate equivalent of a mudstone (not to be confused with a 'mudstone' of the Dunham Limestone classification).  Calcilutites can accumulate in a wide variety of marine and lacustrine environments.
response.summary_2: Activated charcoal (activated carbon) and zeolites are categorized as nanoporous materials with pores generally 100 nanometers or smaller. Activated carbon (activated charcoal) is listed as an example of bulk nanoporous materials, and zeolites are reported to exhibit pore sizes less than a nanometer.
